protocol_definition.name: Voting
protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents answer independently, then cast private ballots; plurality selects the output.
protocol_definition.visibility: Answers are private before voting; voters see all anonymized proposals during voting.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after ballot aggregation; deterministic lowest-index tie break.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Independently produce a complete final-form answer. You cannot see other proposals.
intermediate_messages.content:
1. Plain-Language Definition
A communication protocol is a set of rules that tells multiple AI agents how to talk to each other. It defines the format, timing, and meaning of messages so that every agent understands what to do.

2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
- **What information is shared:** The protocol decides which data or instructions an agent must include in its message, such as a task update or a request for help.
- **When an agent communicates:** It sets the order or trigger for sending messages, like “only speak after receiving a request” or “report results every minute.”

3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
Without a protocol, agents might send conflicting actions—for example, two agents could both try to update the same file at the same time, causing errors. A protocol prevents this by defining clear rules for who acts when, reducing confusion and ensuring smooth teamwork.

4. Real-World Analogy
Think of a restaurant kitchen: the head chef (protocol) decides which cook prepares which dish and when to call out orders. Just as this prevents two cooks from making the same meal or burning food, a communication protocol stops AI agents from duplicating work or missing critical information.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Independently produce a complete final-form answer. You cannot see other proposals.
intermediate_messages.content:
1. Plain-Language Definition
A communication protocol is a set of rules that tells AI agents how to talk to each other. It defines the format, timing, and order of messages so that every agent understands what is being said and when to respond.

2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
- **What information is shared:** The protocol decides which data or requests an agent must include in its message, preventing confusion or missing details.
- **When an agent communicates:** It sets the timing—for example, an agent can only send a message after receiving a specific signal or completing a task.

3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
Without a protocol, agents might send conflicting instructions or duplicate work. For instance, if two agents both try to update the same file without coordinating, they could overwrite each other’s changes. A protocol prevents this by assigning clear responsibilities and a fixed order of actions.

4. Real-World Analogy
Think of a restaurant kitchen: the head chef (protocol) decides who calls out orders (what information), and when the line cooks can start cooking (when to communicate). This prevents two cooks from making the same dish or missing a step, just as a protocol keeps AI agents working smoothly together.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Independently produce a complete final-form answer. You cannot see other proposals.
intermediate_messages.content:
1. Plain-Language Definition
A communication protocol is a set of rules that tells AI agents how to talk to each other. It defines the format, timing, and meaning of messages so that agents can share information and work together without confusion.

2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
- **What information is shared:** The protocol specifies exactly which data or requests an agent must include in its message, preventing irrelevant chatter.
- **When an agent communicates:** It sets the timing—for example, an agent must wait for a specific trigger or response before sending its next message.

3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
Without a protocol, agents might send conflicting instructions or duplicate each other’s work. A protocol reduces **conflicting actions** by ensuring that only one agent acts on a task at a time, keeping the team coordinated and efficient.

4. Real-World Analogy
Think of a restaurant kitchen. The head chef (protocol) decides who calls out an order (what information) and when the line cooks respond (when to communicate). This prevents two cooks from making the same dish or missing a request, just as a protocol keeps AI agents from stepping on each other’s toes.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Independently produce a complete final-form answer. You cannot see other proposals.
intermediate_messages.content:
1. Plain-Language Definition
A communication protocol is a set of rules that tells AI agents how to talk to each other. It defines the format, timing, and meaning of messages so that all agents understand one another and work together smoothly.

2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
- **What information is shared:** The protocol decides which data or results an agent must send to others, preventing irrelevant chatter.
- **When an agent communicates:** It sets the timing—for example, an agent must report its findings before the next agent can start its task.

3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
Without a protocol, agents might duplicate work by both solving the same problem, or they could miss critical information because no one was told to share it. A protocol reduces **conflicting actions** by ensuring agents coordinate their steps, so one agent doesn’t undo another’s progress.

4. Real-World Analogy
Think of a restaurant kitchen. The head chef (protocol) decides that the grill cook sends a “steak ready” message only after the meat is cooked, and the server receives that message before picking up the plate. This prevents cold food and confusion, just as a protocol prevents AI agents from sending messages at the wrong time or sharing useless data.

Task ID: EC-05

Category: Educational Content

Difficulty: Easy

Tool Requirement: Prohibited

Available Tools:
[]

Tool Expectations:
{}

Prompt:
Explain what a communication protocol is in a multi-agent LLM system to a beginner who already understands what an AI agent is but has never studied multi-agent communication.

The explanation must describe what a communication protocol controls and why it is needed when multiple AI agents work together.

Use the following structure:
1. Plain-Language Definition
2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
4. Real-World Analogy

In the second section, identify exactly two elements that a communication protocol may control. Valid examples include which agent communicates, when an agent communicates, what information is shared, which agents can see a message, the order of communication, or when the interaction ends.

In the third section, explain at least one coordination problem that a communication protocol can help reduce, such as duplicated work, missing information, conflicting actions, or unclear responsibilities.

In the final section, use one simple real-world analogy, such as a classroom group project, workplace team, restaurant staff, or sports team, and clearly connect the analogy to communication among AI agents.

Keep the response between 120 and 200 words. Use beginner-friendly language. Do not use external sources, web search, citations, statistics, or unsupported claims about the performance of specific systems.

Required Output Format:
Use the following markdown structure:

1. Plain-Language Definition
2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
4. Real-World Analogy

No prior agent output is visible.

You are acting as role: Researcher.

The head chef (protocol) decides that the grill cook sends a “steak ready” message only after the meat is cooked, and the server receives that message before picking up the plate. This prevents cold food and confusion, just as a protocol prevents AI agents from sending messages at the wrong time or sharing useless data.

The explanation must describe what a communication protocol controls and why it is needed when multiple AI agents work together.

Use the following structure:
1. Plain-Language Definition
2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
4. Real-World Analogy

In the second section, identify exactly two elements that a communication protocol may control. Valid examples include which agent communicates, when an agent communicates, what information is shared, which agents can see a message, the order of communication, or when the interaction ends.

In the third section, explain at least one coordination problem that a communication protocol can help reduce, such as duplicated work, missing information, conflicting actions, or unclear responsibilities.

In the final section, use one simple real-world analogy, such as a classroom group project, workplace team, restaurant staff, or sports team, and clearly connect the analogy to communication among AI agents.

Visible prior context:
[Proposal 1]
1. Plain-Language Definition
A communication protocol is a set of rules that tells multiple AI agents how to talk to each other. It defines the format, timing, and meaning of messages so that every agent understands what to do.

2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
- **What information is shared:** The protocol decides which data or instructions an agent must include in its message, such as a task update or a request for help.
- **When an agent communicates:** It sets the order or trigger for sending messages, like “only speak after receiving a request” or “report results every minute.”

3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
Without a protocol, agents might send conflicting actions—for example, two agents could both try to update the same file at the same time, causing errors. A protocol prevents this by defining clear rules for who acts when, reducing confusion and ensuring smooth teamwork.

4. Real-World Analogy
Think of a restaurant kitchen: the head chef (protocol) decides which cook prepares which dish and when to call out orders. Just as this prevents two cooks from making the same meal or burning food, a communication protocol stops AI agents from duplicating work or missing critical information.

[Proposal 2]
1. Plain-Language Definition
A communication protocol is a set of rules that tells AI agents how to talk to each other. It defines the format, timing, and order of messages so that every agent understands what is being said and when to respond.

2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
- **What information is shared:** The protocol decides which data or requests an agent must include in its message, preventing confusion or missing details.
- **When an agent communicates:** It sets the timing—for example, an agent can only send a message after receiving a specific signal or completing a task.

3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
Without a protocol, agents might send conflicting instructions or duplicate work. For instance, if two agents both try to update the same file without coordinating, they could overwrite each other’s changes. A protocol prevents this by assigning clear responsibilities and a fixed order of actions.

4. Real-World Analogy
Think of a restaurant kitchen: the head chef (protocol) decides who calls out orders (what information), and when the line cooks can start cooking (when to communicate). This prevents two cooks from making the same dish or missing a step, just as a protocol keeps AI agents working smoothly together.

[Proposal 3]
1. Plain-Language Definition
A communication protocol is a set of rules that tells AI agents how to talk to each other. It defines the format, timing, and meaning of messages so that agents can share information and work together without confusion.

2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
- **What information is shared:** The protocol specifies exactly which data or requests an agent must include in its message, preventing irrelevant chatter.
- **When an agent communicates:** It sets the timing—for example, an agent must wait for a specific trigger or response before sending its next message.

3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
Without a protocol, agents might send conflicting instructions or duplicate each other’s work. A protocol reduces **conflicting actions** by ensuring that only one agent acts on a task at a time, keeping the team coordinated and efficient.

4. Real-World Analogy
Think of a restaurant kitchen. The head chef (protocol) decides who calls out an order (what information) and when the line cooks respond (when to communicate). This prevents two cooks from making the same dish or missing a request, just as a protocol keeps AI agents from stepping on each other’s toes.

[Proposal 4]
1. Plain-Language Definition
A communication protocol is a set of rules that tells AI agents how to talk to each other. It defines the format, timing, and meaning of messages so that all agents understand one another and work together smoothly.

2. Two Things a Communication Protocol Controls
- **What information is shared:** The protocol decides which data or results an agent must send to others, preventing irrelevant chatter.
- **When an agent communicates:** It sets the timing—for example, an agent must report its findings before the next agent can start its task.

3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
Without a protocol, agents might duplicate work by both solving the same problem, or they could miss critical information because no one was told to share it. A protocol reduces **conflicting actions** by ensuring agents coordinate their steps, so one agent doesn’t undo another’s progress.

4. Real-World Analogy
Think of a restaurant kitchen. The head chef (protocol) decides that the grill cook sends a “steak ready” message only after the meat is cooked, and the server receives that message before picking up the plate. This prevents cold food and confusion, just as a protocol prevents AI agents from sending messages at the wrong time or sharing useless data.

3. Why Communication Protocols Are Important
Without a protocol, agents might send conflicting instructions or duplicate work. For instance, if two agents both try to update the same file without coordinating, they could overwrite each other’s changes. A protocol prevents this by assigning clear responsibilities and a fixed order of actions.

4. Real-World Analogy
Think of a restaurant kitchen: the head chef (protocol) decides who calls out orders (what information), and when the line cooks can start cooking (when to communicate). This prevents two cooks from making the same dish or missing a step, just as a protocol keeps AI agents working smoothly together.
